Rick Zettler, spoke to her Jun 2009 (two months before his passing) to reference an original I own. Providence on back watercolour on masonite #412 “The Country Lookout” Fred’s place (his neighbour)- Stirling, Ontario. Jack recalled it as one of his earliest works which I refer to as the Red Chair

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Canadian artist (1924–2009)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Jack_Reid_(artist) "Special:EditPage/Jack Reid (artist)") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Jack Reid" artist](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Jack+Reid%22+artist) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Jack+Reid%22+artist+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Jack+Reid%22+artist&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Jack+Reid%22+artist+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Jack+Reid%22+artist) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Jack+Reid%22+artist&acc=on&wc=on) *(June 2011)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

| Jack Reid | |
| --- | --- |
| Born | Jack Reid  1925  [Toronto, Ontario](/wiki/Toronto,_Ontario "Toronto, Ontario"), Canada |
| Died | August 24, 2009(2009-08-24) (aged 83–84)  [Orillia, Ontario](/wiki/Orillia,_Ontario "Orillia, Ontario"), Canada |
| Known for | Watercolourist |

**Jack Reid** (born 1925) was a Canadian watercolour artist.

Born in Toronto, Reid was self-taught, beginning as a [graphic artist](/wiki/Graphic_artist "Graphic artist") until becoming a full-time painter in 1970. He taught workshops and demonstrations and toured worldwide began in 1971, teaching over 11,000 students by the 1990s.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Reid enjoyed a successful career over six decades, with some paintings entering [Her Majesty Queen Elizabeth II's](/wiki/Elizabeth_II_of_the_United_Kingdom "Elizabeth II of the United Kingdom") personal collection at [Windsor Castle](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle"). In 1992, he was awarded the Commemorative Medal by the Canadian government for his contribution to [Canadian art](/wiki/Canadian_art "Canadian art") and was honoured [Arts Person of the Year](/wiki/Brampton_Arts_Council "Brampton Arts Council") in his hometown of [Brampton](/wiki/Brampton "Brampton"). He exhibited in London, England with the [Royal Institute of Painters in Watercolours](/wiki/Royal_Institute_of_Painters_in_Watercolours "Royal Institute of Painters in Watercolours"). He was a lifetime member of [The Arts and Letters Club of Toronto](/wiki/The_Arts_and_Letters_Club_of_Toronto "The Arts and Letters Club of Toronto"), [Visual Arts Brampton](/wiki/Visual_Arts_Brampton "Visual Arts Brampton"), and the [Canadian Society of Painters in Water Colour](/wiki/Canadian_Society_of_Painters_in_Water_Colour "Canadian Society of Painters in Water Colour").

He authored two books, *Watercolour Basics: Let's Get Started* and *Painting Snow and Water*. He produced an instructional [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM") and did a mini-series of painting demos, broadcast on [Rogers Cable](/wiki/Rogers_Cable "Rogers Cable"). In 2001, Jack was featured in the popular American magazine *[Watercolor Magic](/wiki/Watercolor_Magic "Watercolor Magic")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After 33 years of marriage, Reid's wife Maggie died in late 2002. Jack Reid died on August 24, 2009, in [Orillia](/wiki/Orillia "Orillia"), Ontario, at the age of 84.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Jack also was mourned by his companion Pat whom he shared 6 years of joy with.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In 2014, a star on [Brampton](/wiki/Brampton "Brampton")'s Walk of Fame was dedicated to Reid, and was received by nephew Robert McAffee, on his uncle's behalf.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Workshops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jack_Reid_(artist)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Workshops")]

Jack taught thousands of people through workshops in:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* New Brunswick: Steeves Mountain (2006)
* Ontario: Brampton (19??-current), Erieau (2006), Haliburton (2006), Mississauga (2000), Sarnia (2001)
* Saskatchewan: Saskatoon (2005, 2006)
* Alberta: Calgary (2006), Cochrane (2005)
* British Columbia: Agassis (2005), Comox (2005), Kamloops (2005), Qualicum (2006), Victoria & Tofino (2005), Whistler (2007)
* Scotland
* Ireland: Adare (2005), Dingle Peninsula (2005), Dublin (2005), Killarney (2005)
